title: Derivatives of aˣ and logₐx
description: Find the derivatives of simple exponential functions. For example, differentiate f(x)=3⋅2ˣ.
topic_path: Differentiation: composite, implicit, and inverse functions

hints:
The expression for $f(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
f'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(5\cdot2^x)
\\\\
&=5\dfrac{d}{dx}(2^x)
\\\\
&=5\cdot\ln(2)\cdot2^x
\\\\
&=5\cdot2^x\ln(2)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $f'(x)=5\cdot2^x\ln(2)$.

The expression for $g(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
g'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(-4\cdot3^x)
\\\\
&=-4\dfrac{d}{dx}(3^x)
\\\\
&=-4\cdot\ln(3)\cdot3^x
\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $g'(x)=-4\ln(3)\cdot3^x$.

The expression for $h(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
h'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(2\cdot5^x)
\\\\
&=2\dfrac{d}{dx}(5^x)
\\\\
&=2\cdot\ln(5)\cdot5^x
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $h'(x)=2\cdot\ln(5)\cdot 5^x$.

The expression for $f(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
f'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(-8\cdot7^x)
\\\\
&=-8\dfrac{d}{dx}(7^x)
\\\\
&=-8\cdot\ln(7)\cdot7^x
\\\\
&=-8\cdot7^x\ln(7)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $f'(x)=-8\cdot7^x\ln(7)$.

The expression for $g(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
g'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(15\cdot4^x)
\\\\
&=15\dfrac{d}{dx}(4^x)
\\\\
&=15\cdot\ln(4)\cdot4^x
\\\\
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $g'(x)=15 \ln(4)\cdot4^x$.

The expression for $h(x)$ includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
h'(x)&=\dfrac{d}{dx}(-5\cdot6^x)
\\\\
&=-5\dfrac{d}{dx}(6^x)
\\\\
&=-5\cdot\ln(6)\cdot6^x
\\\\
&=-5\cdot 6^x\ln(6)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $h'(x)=-5\cdot 6^x\ln(6)$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(8\cdot3^x)
\\\\
&=8\dfrac{d}{dx}(3^x)
\\\\
&=8\cdot\ln(3)\cdot3^x
\\\\
&=8\cdot3^x\ln(3)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(8\cdot3^x)=8\cdot3^x\ln(3)$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3\cdot2^x)
\\\\
&=-3\dfrac{d}{dx}(2^x)
\\\\
&=-3\cdot\ln(2)\cdot2^x
\\\\
&=2^x\cdot(-3\ln(2))
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3\cdot2^x)=2^x\cdot(-3\ln(2))$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(7\cdot10^x)
\\\\
&=7\dfrac{d}{dx}(10^x)
\\\\
&=7\cdot\ln(10)\cdot10^x
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(7\cdot10^x)=7\ln(10)\cdot 10^x$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(-4\cdot5^x)
\\\\
&=-4\dfrac{d}{dx}(5^x)
\\\\
&=-4\cdot\ln(5)\cdot5^x
\\\\
&=-4\cdot5^x\ln(5)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(-4\cdot5^x)=-4\cdot5^x\ln(5)$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(3\cdot5^x)
\\\\
&=3\dfrac{d}{dx}(5^x)
\\\\
&=3\cdot\ln(5)\cdot5^x
\\\\
&=5^x\cdot 3\ln(5)
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(3\cdot5^x)=5^x\cdot 3\ln(5)$.

The expression to differentiate includes an exponential term.

Remember that the derivative of the general exponential term $a^x$ (where $a$ is any positive constant) is $\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(a^x)=\ln(a)\cdot a^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3\cdot10^x)
\\\\
&=-3\dfrac{d}{dx}(10^x)
\\\\
&=-3\cdot\ln(10)\cdot10^x
\end{align}$
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}(-3\cdot10^x)=-3\ln(10)\cdot10^x$.

The expression for $f(x)$ includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_a(x)]=\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
In conclusion, $f'(x)=\dfrac{5}{\ln(2)x}$.

The expression for $g(x)$ includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_a(x)]=\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
In conclusion, $g'(x)=-\dfrac{4}{x\ln(3)}$.

The expression for $h(x)$ includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_a(x)]=\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
In conclusion, $h'(x)=\dfrac{8}{\ln(5)x}$.

The expression for $f(x)$ includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_a(x)]=\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

The expression to differentiate includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

Put another way, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[\log_a(x)]=\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
In conclusion, $\dfrac{d}{dx}[2\log_5(x)]=\dfrac{2}{\ln(5)x}$.

The expression to differentiate includes a logarithmic term.

Remember that the derivative of the general logarithmic term $\log_a(x)$ (where $a$ is any positive constant and $a\neq 1$) is $\dfrac{1}{\ln(a)\cdot x}$.
